content:
The bug was not in the function. The bug was in the assumption that nobody had challenged before the function existed.

14 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Bad Requirements Create Better Bugs Than Bad CodeSome bugs are easy to blame on code.
A condition was wrong. A query missed a filter. A component stored a stale state. An endpoint trusted the client. A form submitted the wrong payload. A table export ignored the visible filters. A background job retried without idempotency. A permission check lived only in the UI.
Those bugs are real.
But many of them start earlier than the code.
They start with the requirement.
A sentence in a ticket sounds simple. A product conversation feels clear enough. A designer shows the happy path. A stakeholder says, “Just add this option.” A bug report says, “This should work like the other screen.” Someone says, “Users should be able to manage their documents.” Someone else says, “Admins can export reports.”
Everyone nods.
Then, developers build what they think was agreed upon.
The code works exactly as written.
And the system is still wrong.
